title: Graffiti Lives: Beyond the Tag in New York's Urban Underground By Gregory J. Snyder NYU Press. 2011. 252 pages. $20 paper
abstract: Gregory J. Snyder's Graffiti Lives: Beyond the Tag in New York's Urban Underground is a rambunctious and approachable ethnography of serious and not-so-serious NYC graffiti writers that does not bite off more than it can chew. The ambiguous title—is it about the lives of graffiti artists or is it arguing that the graffiti art movement lives?—nicely captures the author's approach, which involved chronicling the lives of the writers (the preferred term among graffiti artists) while at the same time tackling the ways in which graffiti continues to be a part of contemporary urban settings. Rejecting generalizations about graffiti artists and the meaning of graffiti, Snyder describes his work as a “snapshot of a particular period of time,” perhaps a too modest assessment of the book's relevance to broader sociological themes. Snyder touches on a number of larger issues, such as the relationship between graffiti and other criminal activity, the political meaning of graffiti, and ethnographic methods. Among the strengths of the book are candid interviews and accounts of the writers themselves, built upon relationships developed over a ten-year period. Snyder concludes by describing how the skills and experiences of graffiti writers shape them as they age and can be a resource for success in future endeavors and artistic careers.
